Carry invariants in base-$b$ arithmetic (addition/multiplication carries) - Buffed Seed

Let $p=101$, $q=103$ be distinct odd primes and let $k=2$. Define the base
$b:=p^{k}q+1$, and write all integers in base $b$ using digits in $\{0,1,\dots,b-1\}$.

For a prime $\ell$, let $v_{\ell}(n)$ denote the $\ell$-adic valuation (largest $e$ with $\ell^{e}\mid n$).
Let $t$ be the least positive integer such that $p^{2k}q^{2}\mid b^{t}-1$.

Define
$X:=\dfrac{b^{2t}-1}{b^{t}-1}$ and $Y:=b^{t}-1$.
(So $X=1+b^{t}$.)

When adding $X$ and $Y$ in base $b$ with the standard grade-school algorithm, let $c_i$ be the carry sent from
digit position $i$ to $i+1$ (so $c_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$), and define the total carry mass
$C:=\sum_{i\ge 0} c_i$.

Compute the unique $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ such that
$N\equiv b\cdot C+t\pmod{1000}$.
(You must also prove that $t$ exists and determine $t$.)

Solution.
Step 1 (linear_congruence_digit_sum_invariant): Let $n\ge 0$ have base-$b$ expansion
$n=\sum_{i\ge 0} d_i b^i$ with digits $0\le d_i\le b-1$, and define the digit sum $S_b(n):=\sum_{i\ge 0} d_i$.
Since $b\equiv 1\pmod{b-1}$, we have $n\equiv \sum d_i\equiv S_b(n)\pmod{b-1}$.

Step 2 (carry_mass_via_digit_sum_drop): Write $X=\sum x_i b^i$, $Y=\sum y_i b^i$, and $X+Y=\sum z_i b^i$ in base $b$
with $0\le x_i,y_i,z_i\le b-1$. In the grade-school addition there are carries $c_{-1}=0,c_0,c_1,\dots$ (eventually $0$)
such that for every $i\ge 0$:
$x_i+y_i+c_{i-1}=z_i+b\,c_i$.
Summing over all $i$ gives $S_b(X)+S_b(Y)+\sum c_{i-1}=S_b(X+Y)+b\sum c_i$.
Because $c_{-1}=0$ and only finitely many carries are nonzero, $\sum c_{i-1}=\sum c_i=C$, hence
$S_b(X)+S_b(Y)-S_b(X+Y)=(b-1)C$, consistent with Step 1.

Step 3 (base_b_representation): The base-$b$ form of $b^{t}$ is a $1$ followed by $t$ zeros.
Subtracting $1$ forces a borrow chain through all $t$ trailing zeros, so
$Y=b^{t}-1$ has digits $(b-1)$ in positions $0,1,\dots,t-1$ and digit $0$ in position $t$.
Also $X=1+b^{t}$ has digit $1$ in position $0$, digit $1$ in position $t$, and zeros elsewhere.

Step 4 (parity_invariant): Since $p,q$ are odd and $k\ge 2$, we have $p^{k}q\ge 3^{2}\cdot 5=45$, hence $b=p^{k}q+1\ge 46$.
In particular $b>2$, so the digit $2$ is a valid base-$b$ digit and cannot itself trigger a further carry.

Step 5 (prime_power_lifting_step): (LTE) If $r$ is an odd prime with $r\mid(a-b)$ and $r\nmid ab$, then for all $n\ge 1$,
$v_r(a^{n}-b^{n})=v_r(a-b)+v_r(n)$.
Here $a=b$ and $b=1$. Since $p\mid(b-1)$ and $p\nmid b$, LTE applies and yields
$v_p(b^{n}-1)=v_p(b-1)+v_p(n)=k+v_p(n)$ because $v_p(b-1)=v_p(p^{k}q)=k$.
Similarly, LTE applies at $q$ and yields $v_q(b^{n}-1)=v_q(b-1)+v_q(n)=1+v_q(n)$ because $v_q(b-1)=1$.
(We do not apply LTE at $2$ since $2\mid b$ makes the hypothesis $2\nmid ab$ fail.)

Step 6 (p_adic_valuation): The condition $p^{2k}q^{2}\mid b^{t}-1$ is equivalent to
$v_p(b^{t}-1)\ge 2k$ and $v_q(b^{t}-1)\ge 2$.
Using the valuation formulas from Step 5:
$k+v_p(t)\ge 2k$ so $v_p(t)\ge k$, and $1+v_q(t)\ge 2$ so $v_q(t)\ge 1$.
Thus $p^{k}\mid t$ and $q\mid t$, i.e. $p^{k}q\mid t$.

Step 7 (inequality_bounds_for_integers): Let $t_0:=p^{k}q$. Then $v_p(t_0)=k$ and $v_q(t_0)=1$, so from Step 5 we get
$v_p(b^{t_0}-1)=k+k=2k$ and $v_q(b^{t_0}-1)=1+1=2$, hence $p^{2k}q^{2}\mid b^{t_0}-1$.
By the divisibility constraint from Step 6, every qualifying $t$ is a multiple of $t_0$, so the least such $t$ is uniquely $t=t_0$.
Numerically, $t=101^{2}\cdot 103=1050703$, and $b=t+1=1050704$.

Step 8 (constraints_on_representation): From the digit descriptions from Step 3 and the bound $b>2$ from Step 4:
$S_b(X)=2$, $S_b(Y)=t(b-1)$, and $X+Y=(1+b^{t})+(b^{t}-1)=2b^{t}$ has a single digit $2$ in position $t$, so $S_b(X+Y)=2$.

Step 9 (carry_invariants): Plug the digit sums from Step 8 into the carry identity from Step 2:
$(b-1)C=S_b(X)+S_b(Y)-S_b(X+Y)=2+t(b-1)-2=t(b-1)$, hence $C=t$.
So $C=1050703$ from Step 7.

Step 10 (prime_power_to_carry_bridge): The required congruence is
$N\equiv b\cdot C+t\pmod{1000}$.
Using $C=t$ from Step 9 gives $N\equiv t(b+1)\pmod{1000}$.
Using $b=t+1$ from Step 7 gives $N\equiv t(t+2)\pmod{1000}$.

Step 11 (linear_congruence_mod_b_minus_1): Reduce the expression from Step 10 modulo $1000$.
From Step 7, $t=1050703\equiv 703\pmod{1000}$, so
$N\equiv 703\cdot 705\pmod{1000}$.
Compute $703\cdot 705=703\cdot 700+703\cdot 5=492100+3515=495615$, hence $N\equiv 615\pmod{1000}$.

Step 12 (closure_certificate): The normalization demands the unique residue $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$, so from Step 11 we get $N=615$.
Answer: 615

3) Uniqueness Test
- Uniqueness of $t$: Step 6 forces $p^{k}q\mid t$ for any exponent satisfying $p^{2k}q^{2}\mid b^{t}-1$, and Step 7 shows
$t_0=p^{k}q$ itself works. Hence the least such exponent is uniquely $t=t_0$.
- Uniqueness of $C$: For fixed $b,X,Y$, the grade-school addition algorithm is deterministic, so the carry sequence $(c_i)$ and
$C=\sum c_i$ are unique; Step 9 then forces $C=t$.
- Uniqueness of $N$: With $b,C,t$ fixed, $bC+t$ is fixed, and reduction modulo $1000$ has a unique representative in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

615
